content.full_text:
Re
Farn Polrucal Pnacrtcns CouvtISSIoN
P.O. Box tt07 . -128 J Strect . Srcraurerrto, CA 95ltll-0t107
(9 l6) -12l--5660 . Fax t9l6) .i21-08tt6
December 11, 2000
Paul A. Billeci
Western Regional Director
CiviGenics,Inc.
77 MoragaWay, Suite G
Orinda, CA 94563
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-234
Dear Mr. Billeci:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should
be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place.2 Further,
this letter is based on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Commission does
not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) t FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Given that two large contracts issued by the California Department of
Corrections' Office of Substance Abuse Program are coming up for re-bid in the next few
months, and the company you now work for, CiviGenics, Inc., plans to bid on both -
Request for Proposals, do you need to be able to prove to another bidder that has
threatened to file a protest, that you are not violating the post-employment restrictions of
the Act?
t Goue-ment Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18996, ofthe California Code ofRegulations. Because the question you pose seeks general advice,
we are issuing informal assistance as allowed by section 83 114 and regulation 18329. Please be aware that
informal assistance does not confer upon the requestor the immunity of formal advice. (Section 831 14 and
regulation 1E329.)
2 Pursuant to regulation 18329(bX8XA), the Commission does not provide advice regarding past
conduct.


Ow File No. I-00-234
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
We cannot advise you as to what you may or may not need to do in the context of
threatened bid protest litigation. We can only outline the post-employment restrictions of
the Act as they may be applicable to your situation.
FACTS
You retired from the California Department of Corrections ("CDC") in April of
2000. You served as a Correctional Program Manager for the Department's Office of
Substance Abuse Programs ("OSAP") from 1990 until retirement. In that capaciU, You
developed program policies and procedures for OSAP and managed the contracts for a
number of in-prison substance abuse treatment programs.
Upon your retirement from CDC, you went to work for CiviGenics, Inc., as the
Western Regional Director. Although CiviGenics is currently an OSAP contractor, you
did not manage any of their contracts and did not participate in the competitive bid
process in which they were selected as a CDC contractor.
In your current capacity with CiviGenics, Inc., your duties include regional
planning, the advancement of business opportunities and progftIm expansion, non-profit
management and mergers, property and facility development, the coordination of
information responding to government solicitations, new contract development and
negotiations, operations activation and over site. In addition, you are responsible for the
administrative support and management of the criminal justice treatment projects and
operations within the 12-state region.
Prior to leaving state service, you were advised by CDC personnel that although
you could retire and be employed by CiviGenics, Inc., you could not be involved in
contract negotiations and decisions with the Department for 12 months following your
departure from state service. You have complied with that requirement, consistent with
Government Code $87406(d) since leaving and will continue to do so.
Two large OSAP contracls are coming up for re-bid in the coming months. .-
CiviGenics, Inc. plans to bid on both Request for Proposals (RFP). You fully intend to
have nothing to do with any aspect of the CiviGenics, Inc. proposal per the Government
Code. However, you have been wamed by one of the contractors whose $4 million a
year contract is being re-bid, that if CiviGencics, Inc. wins the competitive bid process
during the RFP process, they may file a protest based upon your employment with
CiviGenics,Inc.
You request advice from the FPPC as to whether you need to be able to prove that
you are not violating the Government Code, and if so, how?


Our File No. I-00-234
Page No. 3
AIIALYSN
The Act in no way precludes you from accepting a position in the private sector
upon leaving state service. There is no problem under the Act with your working for
CiviGenics, Inc., after you retired from the CDC, so long as you abide by the post-
employment restrictions of the Act.
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-
govemmental employment restrictions under the Act. The first is a permanent
prohibition on advising or representing any person for compensation in any judicial or
other proceeding (including contracts) in which the official participated while in state
service. The second is a one-year ban on making any appearance before their former
agency for compensation for the purpose of influencing any administrative, legislative or
other specified action (including contracts). As a state administrative official and a
designated employee with the CDC, your future activities are limited, as applicable, by
both the permanent and one year bans.
Permanent Ban on "Switching Sides"
Section 87 401 provides:
"No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or
otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State
of California) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any
formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or
written communication with the intent to influence, in
connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding if both of the following apply:
(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and
substantial interest.
(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state
administrative official participated."
Section 87402 provides
"No former state adminishative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office shall
for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist in
representing any other person (except the State of
California) in any proceeding in which the official would
be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401."


Our File No. I-00-234
Page No. 4
Sections 87401 and.87402 prohibit former state administrative officials,3 who
participated in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding while employed by a state
agency, from being paid to represent or assist in representing another person regarding
that same proceeding. A'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" includes, "any
proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim,
controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, aJTest or other particular matter involving
a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency ...." (Section
87a00(c).) Because a govemmental RFP is integral to the execution of a governmental
contract whereby specific parties will be identified, we have advised in the past that a
governmental RFP is a "proceeding" under section 87400(c). (Ferber Advice Letter, No.
l-99-104; and Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) Thus, under the permanent ban,
you may not, for compensation represent CiviGenics,Inc., before the CDC in any RFP
proceeding in which you participated while you worked for the CDC.
An official is considered to have "participated" in a contract or proceeding, if the
official was personally and substantially involved in the contract or proceeding. (Section
87400(d).) A former state official who held a management position in a state
administrative agency is deemed to have participated in a contract or proceeding if: 1)
the contract or proceeding was pending before the agency during his or her tenure, and2)
any decision regarding the contract or proceeding was made by the official directly or by
someone under his or her supervision. (Regulation 187a1.1(a)(a).)a
The permanent ban applies throughout the duration of a proceeding in which the
official participated. The permanent ban does not, however, apply to "new" proceedings
in which the former employee did not participate. (Section 87401; Pratt Advice Letter,
No. 4-95-3 86; Glaab Advice Letter, No. A-97-3 4l; Anderson, supra, and Grady Advice
Letter, No. I-99-034.)
A new RFP covering a new contract that is based on new consideration and new
terms, even if involving the same parties, would be a "new" proceeding for purposes of
section 87400. (Ferber and. Anderson, supra.) If an RFP being sent out for re-bid is
substantially the same as a current RFP, however, we have previously advised that the
two RFP's will be considered the same proceeding for purposes of the permanent ban.
(Ferber and Anderson, supra.) -.
' A "state administrative official" is defined in Section 87400(b) as "every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities
engages in any judicial, quasijudicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or
ministerial capacity." The California Departrnent of Corrections is a state administative agency as defined
in Section 87400(b). As a Correctional Program Manager for the CDC's Office of Substance Abuse
Programs, you were a state administrative official.
a Regulation 187a1.1(a)(a) refers to Regulation 18438.2 for the definition of "pending."
Regulation 18438.2(bxl) states that a proceeding is pending before an age\cy "[w]hen the ... proceeding
has been commenced ...."


Our File No. I-00-234
Page No. 5
As to ongoing work on a contract, the Act's permanent ban prohibits you from
performing work for a private employer on the same contract that you performed
substantial work on in your position with the Departmentof Corrections. (Glaab Advice
Letter, supra.) However, the permanent ban does not prohibit you from performing work
on contracts that you did not work on as an employee of the Department of Corrections,
or on new contracts between your employer and the Department of Corrections. In
addition, the Commission considers the application, drafting and awarding of a contract
to be a separate proceeding from the monitoring and performance of the contract.
(Blonten Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) Therefore, if you had not worked on the
monitoring and performance of a certain contract at the Department, you are not
prohibited from working on the performance of that contract at CiviGenics, Inc.
One-Year Ban
In addition to the permanent ban, the Act prohibits specified officials, for a period
of one year after leaving state service, from being paid to communicate with or appear
before their former agency for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative
action, or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, contract or the sale of goods or property. Section
87406(dX1 ) specifically provides that:
"No designated employees of a state administrative
agency ... for a period of one year after leaving office or
employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or
attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by
making any formal or informal appearance, or by making
any oral or written communication, before any state
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for
which he or she worked or represented during the 12
months before leaving office or employment, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendrnent, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grffit, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods
or property. For purposes ofthis paragraph, an appearance
before a state administrative agency does not include an
appearance in a court of law, before an administrative law
judge, or before the Workers' Compensation Appeals
Board."
5 Regulation B7a6.L(a)(2) clarifres section 87406(d)(1) to include any employee of a state
administrativ,e agency who holds a position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's
conflict of interest code. As a Correctional Program Manager for OSAP, you are included within the
coverage of section 87406(d)(1).


Our File No. I-00-234
Page No. 6
An employee's "former agency" includes any state administrative agency she or
he "worked for or represented" during the l2-month period before she or he left state
service and also includes any agency whose budget, personnel and other operations are
controlled by the former agency. (Regulation 187a6.1(a)(6).)
The one year ban prohibits a former state employee, for a period of one year after
leaving state service, from being paid to in/luence his or her former agency either orally
or in writing. Regulation 18746.2 defines "influencing" under Section 87406 as oral or
written communication:
"[M]ade for the principal purpose of supporting,
promoting, influencing, modiffing, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding. An appearance or
communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing
by telephone or in person, corresponding with in writing or
by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and
delivering or sending any communication."
Under Section 87406(d)(1), a former employee may not "influence" the former agency
with respect to actions or proceedings to, among other things, issue, award or amend a
contract. Thus, you are prohibited from responding to any RFP from the CDC or OSAP
for a period of 12 months after you left state service.
With respect to performance of a contract under the one year ban, regulation
18746.1(b)(sXA) provides that the one year ban will not be violated if the former
employee is engaging in services to administer, implement, or fulfiIIthe requirements of
an existing contract, provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of the existing contract (and that the provisions of the permanent
ban do not apply to that contract, as discussed above).
Enclosed for your information, is a fact sheet concerning the post-employment
restrictions of the Act. If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
W. ? ,,1,,
By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
HPWjg ,

Ow Page No. 2

the Act as they may be applicable to your situation.
